content:
# Genesis Chapter 50 — PNEUMA (Dynamic Equivalence) Translation

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 29r–29v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_050_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_050_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054 (page 50), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0055 (page 51)

---

*A Son's Grief*

**50:1** Joseph threw himself on his father's face and wept over him and kissed him. **50:2** Then Joseph ordered his servants — the physicians — to embalm his father Israel. So the physicians embalmed him, **50:3** taking the full forty days required for it, for that is the full period of embalming. And Egypt mourned for him seventy days.

---

*The Funeral Procession to Canaan*

**50:4** When the days of mourning for him had passed, Joseph spoke to Pharaoh's household, saying: "If I have found favor with you, please speak these words in Pharaoh's hearing: **50:5** 'My father made me swear an oath. He said: "I am about to die. Bury me in my grave that I dug for myself in the land of Canaan." Now, please let me go up and bury my father, and then I will return.'"

**50:6** Pharaoh said, "Go up and bury your father, just as he made you swear to do."

**50:7** So Joseph went up to bury his father. All of Pharaoh's officials went with him — the elders of his household, and all the elders of the land of Egypt — **50:8** along with all of Joseph's household, his brothers, and his father's household. Only their little children, their flocks, and their herds were left behind in Goshen. **50:9** Both chariots and horsemen went up with him — it was an enormous company.

**50:10** They came to the threshing floor of Atad, which is beyond the Jordan, and there they held a great and solemn lamentation. Joseph performed a mourning rite for his father that lasted seven days. **50:11** When the Canaanites who lived in that region saw the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad, they said, "This is a grave mourning for the Egyptians." That is why the place was named Abel-Mizraim — *Mourning of Egypt* — it is still beyond the Jordan today.

**50:12** So Jacob's sons did for him exactly what he had commanded them: **50:13** his sons carried him to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, near Mamre, the field that Abraham had purchased as a burial site from Ephron the Hittite.

---

*The Brothers' Fear*

**50:14** After he had buried his father, Joseph returned to Egypt — he and his brothers and all who had gone up with him for the burial.

**50:15** When Joseph's brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, "What if Joseph holds a grudge against us and pays us back for all the harm we did to him?" **50:16** So they sent word to Joseph, saying: "Before your father died, he gave this instruction: **50:17** 'Say this to Joseph: Please forgive the transgression of your brothers and the sin they committed — the wrong they did to you.' Now then, please forgive the transgression of the servants of your father's God."

When this message reached him, Joseph broke down and wept.

**50:18** Then his brothers came and fell down before him. "We are your slaves," they said.

---

*Joseph's Answer: The Great Reversal*

**50:19** But Joseph said to them, "Do not be afraid. Am I in the place of God? **50:20** You intended to harm me — but God intended it for good, to accomplish what is now being done: the saving of many lives. **50:21** So then, don't be afraid. I will provide for you and your little ones." And he comforted them and spoke kindly to them.

---

*Joseph's Final Days and Death*

**50:22** Joseph lived in Egypt, he and his father's household. Joseph lived one hundred and ten years. **50:23** Joseph saw the third generation of Ephraim's children. The children of Machir son of Manasseh were also born on Joseph's knees.

**50:24** Then Joseph said to his brothers, "I am about to die. But God will surely come to your aid — God will certainly visit you — and bring you up out of this land to the land he promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob."

**50:25** And Joseph made the sons of Israel take an oath. He said: "When God comes to your aid, you must carry my bones up from this place."

**50:26** So Joseph died at the age of one hundred and ten years. They embalmed him, and he was laid in a coffin in Egypt.

---

## Meaning Notes

### v. 1 — The Grief of Joseph

The Hebrew **וַיִּפֹּל יוֹסֵף עַל-פְּנֵי אָבִיו** (*wayyippol Yosef al-penei aviv*) — "Joseph fell upon the face of his father" — is a posture of overwhelming grief, the body collapsing onto the beloved dead. It is not ceremonial; it is raw. The last thing Jacob experienced before Genesis 49 was Joseph's hand pressing on his eyes (48:12 — though the text does not spell this out explicitly, the parallel is felt). Now Joseph presses his face to his father's face. The intimacy is total.

---

### v. 2 — Embalming

Embalming (*chanit*, from the root **חנט**, *chanat*) was an Egyptian, not an Israelite, practice. The body was preserved through a long drying and wrapping process. That Jacob is embalmed marks the extent to which Egypt has become the family's world — they operate within its customs even as they resist full assimilation. The physicians (*rophe'im*) who perform the embalming are Joseph's own servants, not the priestly embalmers who normally performed this function for royalty; perhaps the text signals that Jacob receives royal treatment, or perhaps it simply reflects that Joseph's household had medical expertise at its disposal.

---

### v. 3 — Forty Days, Seventy Days

The forty days for embalming and the seventy-day mourning period are both significant numbers. Forty (*arba'im*) recurs throughout the Bible as the duration of transformation and ordeal: forty days of flood, forty years in the wilderness, forty days of Moses on the mountain. Seventy (*shiv'im*) is the number of completeness and fullness: the seventy members of Jacob's family who entered Egypt (46:27), the seventy elders of Israel. Egypt mourning for Jacob seventy days — nearly as long as the mourning for a pharaoh — is a remarkable social fact. Jacob has become, through Joseph, a figure of Egyptian as well as Israelite significance.

---

### v. 5 — The Grave Jacob Dug

**אֲשֶׁר כָּרִיתִי לִי** (*asher kariti li*, "which I dug for myself") is an interesting detail: Jacob claims to have personally prepared a tomb in Canaan. No such act is narrated elsewhere in Genesis. Some commentators take this as Joseph's diplomatic phrasing, softening the actual command (Genesis 49:29–32 — "bury me in the cave of Machpelah") into the more personal, emotional language of a man who has prepared his own resting place. Others take it literally, imagining an act of preparation not recorded by the narrative. Either way, the image is powerful: an old man digging in anticipation of his own death, insisting on his homeland even from the far country of Egypt.

---

### v. 10–11 — Abel-Mizraim

**אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם** (*Abel Mitzrayim*) — the name plays on two meanings of **אָבֵל**: it can mean "mourning" (*evel*, the noun of grief) and it is also a place-name element meaning "meadow" or "watercourse" (found in place names like Abel-Beth-Maacah, Abel-Shittim). The double resonance is deliberate: a meadow becomes a field of mourning; a geographic marker becomes a memorial to grief. Egypt weeping in Canaan — the procession has, in a sense, brought Egypt itself to the Promised Land, even if only to mourn.

---

### v. 15 — "What If Joseph Holds a Grudge"

The brothers' fear upon Jacob's death reveals what their father's presence had provided: a buffer, a protector, an implicit guarantee of Joseph's restraint. With Jacob gone, they believe the calculus changes. Their words — **לוּ שָׂטְמֵנוּ יוֹסֵף** (*lu satmenu Yosef*, "What if Joseph hates/bears enmity toward us") — use the verb **שָׂטַם** (*satam*), the same root used in 49:23 for the archers who "attacked" Joseph. It is a word for sustained, persisting hostility, the grudge that does not forget. Their fear is theologically important: they do not yet fully trust that forgiveness has truly happened. Forgiveness announced (Genesis 45) does not always feel secure. The deepest wound fears the deepest re-opening.

---

### v. 17 — Pesha: Transgression, Not Mistake

**פֶּשַׁע** (*pesha*) — this word must not be softened. It is the strongest term in the Hebrew vocabulary for willful rebellion and moral violation: deliberate defection from a right relationship, a conscious crossing of a line that was known to be a line. It appears throughout the prophets for Israel's covenant violations (Isaiah 1:2; Amos 2:1). The brothers did not make an error in judgment; they committed a *pesha* — a deliberate act of betrayal against both their brother and the God who ordered the family. That they use this word about themselves shows a genuine reckoning with what they did. Joseph's response — weeping — is his answer before any words are spoken.

---

### v. 19 — "Am I in the Place of God?"

**הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנִי** (*ha-tachat Elohim ani*) — this rhetorical question is not a deflection. It is a profound theological statement disguised as a question. Joseph is not saying "I could not punish you even if I wanted to." He is saying: "Vengeance and final judgment belong to God alone. I have no business standing in that place." The preposition **תַּחַת** (*tachat*, "in the place of, beneath, instead of") positions Joseph as consciously stepping back from divine prerogative. This is not weakness but wisdom — the refusal to usurp the role of judge. It echoes Deuteronomy 32:35 ("Vengeance is mine") and anticipates the New Testament ethic of Romans 12:19. Joseph has learned that the one who suffers wrongly is not therefore authorized to retaliate. That authority belongs to God.

---

### v. 20 — The Theological Center of the Joseph Narrative

**וְאַתֶּם חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה אֱלֹהִים חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה** (*we'attem chashavtem alai ra'ah, Elohim chashavah letovah*) —

"You *intended* to harm me — but God *intended* it for good."

The key verb is **חָשַׁב** (*chashav*) — to think, to plan, to reckon, to devise, to weave together (as a weaver calculates a pattern). It is the vocabulary of intentionality, of purposive thought that aims at an outcome. The same verb, the same word, is applied twice in the same sentence to diametrically opposed subjects: the brothers and God. Both were *thinking*, both were *planning*, both were *weaving* — but toward opposite ends.

This is the doctrine of providence expressed with maximum compression and maximum shock. There is no minimizing of what the brothers did: **רָעָה** (*ra'ah*) — evil, harm, disaster — is the plainest word for wickedness. They meant to destroy. And yet — **אֱלֹהִים חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה** — God was weaving the same events toward good. Not that God approved the evil. Not that the brothers' guilt is diminished. But that God's sovereign purpose ran through, beneath, and beyond the human crime — so that what the brothers tore apart, God was stitching into a garment of salvation.

**לְמַעַן עֲשֹׂה כַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה לְהַחֲיֹת עַם-רָב** (*lema'an asoh kayyom hazeh lehachayot am rav*) — "to accomplish what is now being done: the saving of many lives." The phrase **כַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה** (*kayyom hazeh*, "as it is this very day") is vivid: Joseph gestures at the present moment — Egypt fed, the ancient world preserved, his family alive before him — as the concrete evidence of the invisible divine purpose that ran through his suffering. The "many lives" (*am rav*, literally "a numerous people") is not abstract; it is the people standing in the room and the people throughout Egypt and Canaan who ate because Joseph was in Egypt.

This verse is the theological heart of the entire Joseph narrative, and arguably one of the most important single verses in Genesis. It does not explain suffering. It does not make it painless. It insists that God's weaving goes deeper than human wickedness.

---

### v. 21 — "Spoke Kindly to Them"

**וַיְנַחֵם אוֹתָם וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לִבָּם** (*wayenahem otam waydabber al-libbam*) — "he comforted them and spoke to their hearts." The phrase **דִּבֶּר עַל-לֵב** (*dibber al-lev*, "to speak upon/to the heart") is the language of deep reassurance, of speaking past the surface of words into the core of a person's fear and need. It is used of Shechem speaking to Dinah after the assault (34:3 — in that context with disturbing irony), of Boaz to Ruth (2:13), of the Levite to his concubine (Judges 19:3). It always implies tenderness directed toward someone who is in pain or distress. Joseph does not merely issue a formal pardon; he moves toward his brothers' inner wound and addresses it directly. This is reconciliation, not just resolution.

---

### v. 24 — Paqod Yifqod: The Divine Visitation

**פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם** (*paqod yifqod Elohim etkhem*) — the infinitive absolute construction **פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד** intensifies the verb to the point of certainty: "God will surely, certainly, without question come to your aid / visit you." The root **פָּקַד** (*paqad*) is one of the richest and most theologically loaded verbs in Biblical Hebrew: to visit, to attend to, to appoint, to muster, to take account of, to remember with purposive attention. When God *paqads* his people, it means he has turned toward them with intent — for rescue, for judgment, or for commissioning. In Exodus 3:16 and 4:31, this exact formula — **פָּקֹד פָּקַדְתִּי** (*paqod paqadti*, "I have surely visited you") — is God's announcement to Moses and to the elders that the time of rescue has come. Joseph's dying words here are therefore a prophetic anticipation: he has heard what God said to him in his bones before the Exodus story has even begun. He dies pointing forward.

---

### v. 25 — The Oath of the Bones

Joseph does not merely express a wish; he binds his descendants with an oath. **וַיַּשְׁבַּע יוֹסֵף אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*wayyashba Yosef et-benei Yisrael*) — "Joseph made the sons of Israel swear." This oath becomes a living thread binding generations: it surfaces again in Exodus 13:19, where Moses takes Joseph's bones when Israel departs Egypt, and again in Joshua 24:32, where those bones are finally buried in Shechem. Joseph's demand is an act of faith: he insists that Egypt, however secure and prosperous, is not home. The bones must go up. The story is not over. The promise is not yet fulfilled, but it will be.

---

### v. 26 — The Coffin in Egypt

**וַיִּשִׂימוּ אֹתוֹ בָּאָרוֹן בְּמִצְרָיִם** (*wayyasimu oto va'aron bemitzrayim*) — "they placed him in a coffin in Egypt."

This is the last sentence of Genesis, and it is an ending that refuses to close. A coffin in Egypt: preserved, waiting, not yet home. The word **אָרוֹן** (*aron*) — coffin here, the same word used later for the *Ark* of the Covenant — holds the body of the man who represents Israel's whole story. He believed the promise. He extracted an oath. He died still in the foreign land. And his bones lie in a box, ready to travel when the time comes.

Genesis ends not with arrival but with anticipation — not with rest but with waiting. The coffin is both a tomb and a promise. Israel is in Egypt. God has not forgotten them. The story will continue.

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus)

### v. 3 — Mourning Duration

The LXX (Vaticanus) follows the MT in recording forty days for embalming and seventy days of mourning, with no significant textual divergence.

### v. 5 — "The Grave I Dug"

The LXX renders **אֲשֶׁר כָּרִיתִי לִי** as **ὃν ὤρυξα ἐμαυτῷ** (*hon oryksa emautoi*, "which I dug for myself"), closely following the MT. Vaticanus shows no variant here.

### v. 10 — Threshing Floor / Mourning Ground

The LXX renders **גֹּרֶן הָאָטָד** (*goren ha'atad*, "threshing floor of Atad") as **ἅλων Ἀτάδ** (*halon Atad*) — threshing floor of Atad — matching MT. However, the Greek adds **πένθος μέγα καὶ ἰσχυρόν** (*penthos mega kai ischyron*, "great and mighty grief / lamentation") which is slightly more emphatic than the MT's **מִסְפֵּד גָּדֹל** (*misped gadol*, "great mourning"). The intensification in Greek gives additional emotional weight.

### v. 11 — Abel-Mizraim

The LXX renders the name as **Πένθος Αἰγύπτου** (*Penthos Aigyptou*, "Mourning of Egypt"), explicitly translating rather than transliterating the Hebrew — a LXX tendency throughout to explain etymologies within the text rather than preserve them as place-names.

### v. 20 — The Theological Crux

The LXX renders **חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה** as **ὑμεῖς ἐβουλεύσασθε κατ' ἐμοῦ εἰς πονηρά** (*hymeis ebouleusasthe kat' emou eis ponera*, "you plotted evil against me") and **אֱלֹהִים חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה** as **ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἐβουλεύσατο περὶ ἐμοῦ εἰς ἀγαθά** (*ho de theos ebouleusato peri emou eis agatha*, "but God planned concerning me for good"). The use of **βουλεύομαι** (*bouleuomai*, "to deliberate, to plan, to counsel") mirrors the MT's *chashav* precisely — both human deliberation and divine deliberation are expressed with the same Greek verb, preserving the stunning theological parallel of the Hebrew. Vaticanus here shows no deviation from the expected LXX tradition; the translation is faithful and theologically careful.

### v. 22 — Joseph's Age and Children

The LXX at verse 22 agrees with MT that Joseph lived 110 years. Vaticanus shows no textual expansion here. In some manuscript traditions there is a tendency to add details about Joseph's descendants, but Vaticanus is restrained.

### v. 24 — Paqod Yifqod

The LXX renders the emphatic double verb **פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד** as **ἐπισκοπῇ ἐπισκέψεται** (*episkopei episkepsetai*, "by visitation he will surely visit") — using the cognate-noun reinforcement pattern in Greek to match the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction. **ἐπισκέπτομαι** (*episkeptomai*, "to visit, to look upon with care, to inspect") is the standard LXX equivalent for *paqad* throughout the Pentateuch, and it carries the same dual valence in Greek: oversight, visitation, rescue. The Vaticanus preserves this exactly.

### v. 26 — The Final Verse in LXX

Vaticanus closes Genesis 50 with **καὶ ἔθηκαν αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ σορῷ ἐν Αἰγύπτῳ** (*kai ethēkan auton en tē sorō en Aigyptō*, "and they placed him in the sarcophagus/coffin in Egypt"). The word **σορός** (*soros*) in classical Greek originally meant an urn or container for bones, later generalized to any coffin or sarcophagus. It is a fitting word for a text that insists that Joseph's bones have not found their final resting place. The LXX ending is identical in force to the MT: the coffin, the foreign land, the unresolved expectation.

---

## Note on the Ending of Genesis

Genesis does not end with a homecoming. That is the point.

The book that began with creation — light, sky, land, sea, living things, the man and the woman in the garden — ends with a coffin in Egypt. The trajectory from Eden to Egypt is one of exile, fracture, and faithful waiting. What the book insists upon, across fifty chapters, is that God works through exactly this: through the fractures, through the exiles, through the deaths of people who did not receive what they were promised, through the long wait between oath and fulfillment.

Abraham died owning only a field and a cave. Isaac died blind and deceived. Jacob died in a foreign country. Joseph dies in Egypt, having done everything — risen to the heights, fed the world, forgiven his brothers, received his father's blessing, seen his grandchildren — and yet his last act is to bind his descendants with a demand: *take my bones home*. He knows, as they must learn to know, that Egypt is not home. The story is not done.

The coffin in Egypt is the last image of Genesis because Genesis is, at its deepest level, not a book of arrivals but of promises. Creation is promised back to the creatures who broke it. A land is promised to a man who wanders it as a foreigner. A people is promised to a couple whose bodies are as good as dead. And now, at the close, a future is promised to a family locked in a foreign empire. The coffin will move. The bones will go up. The Exodus is already inside this final sentence, waiting.

And so Genesis ends — not with a tomb sealed shut, but with a box set down for now, pointed toward a land it has not yet reached.
